knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, columns are the vertical entities within a table that store and organize data. They define the structure of each row and can be used to filter, sort, and group data.
what_you_will_learn: Data Types — defining column data types (e.g., integer, varchar, timestamp) and their characteristics (e.g., precision, scale)
Column Constraints — adding constraints to columns (e.g., NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY, UNIQUE) for data integrity
Default Values — setting default values for columns to provide a fallback when no value is specified
Auto-Incrementing Columns — using sequences or identity columns to automatically assign unique values to rows
Computed Columns — creating virtual columns that calculate their values based on other columns or expressions
Column Aliases — assigning temporary names to columns in queries for easier referencing and formatting
Column Ordering — specifying the order of columns within a table for better data organization and querying

knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, rows are the individual records stored in a table, consisting of one or more columns. Each row represents a single entry in the table and can be thought of as a tuple of values.
what_you_will_learn: Row ID — a unique identifier assigned to each row within a table
Tuples — representing rows as ordered collections of values, used for data manipulation and storage
Row locking — acquiring exclusive access to a row for modifications or updates
Row-level security (RLS) — controlling access to specific rows based on user permissions and roles
Row versioning — tracking changes made to individual rows over time using PostgreSQL's MVCC feature
Materialized views — pre-computed tables that store the result of a query, including row-level data

knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, data types define the structure and format of data stored in tables, including numeric, string, date/time, and other types.
what_you_will_learn: Integer — a whole number type with optional precision (e.g., int4 for 32-bit integer)
Numeric — an arbitrary-precision decimal or floating-point type
Character Varying (CV) / Character Large Object (CLO) — variable-length string types, including UTF-8 support
Boolean — a logical true/false value type
Date/Time — date and time types with various formats (e.g., timestamp, timestamptz, interval)
Array — an ordered collection of values of the same data type
JSONB — binary JSON type for efficient storage and querying of complex data structures
Enum — a set of named values that can be used as a single value in queries

knowledge.what_it_is: Queries are SQL statements that retrieve or manipulate data from PostgreSQL databases, allowing developers to interact with the database and extract insights.
what_you_will_learn: SELECT statements — retrieving specific columns and rows from a table using various clauses (WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT, etc.)
INSERT statements — adding new records to a table, including support for ON CONFLICT and RETURNING clauses
UPDATE statements — modifying existing records in a table, with options for partial updates and WHERE clauses
DELETE statements — removing records from a table, including support for CASCADE and RESTRICT constraints
JOINs — combining rows from two or more tables based on common columns, including INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL OUTER JOINs
Subqueries — using SELECT statements within other queries to filter or aggregate data
Common Table Expressions (CTEs) — defining temporary result sets that can be referenced within a query
Window functions — applying calculations across rows that are related to the current row, including ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), and LAG() functions

knowledge.what_it_is: A relational database is a type of database that organizes data into one or more tables with predefined schemas, allowing for efficient querying and manipulation of structured data. PostgreSQL is an open-source implementation of the SQL standard, widely used for enterprise-level applications due to its reliability, scalability, and extensibility.
what_you_will_learn: Database schema — defining table relationships using foreign keys, primary keys, and constraints
Entity-relationship modeling (ERM) — designing database structures based on real-world entities and their interactions
Normalization — organizing data to minimize data redundancy and improve data integrity through techniques like 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF
Denormalization — intentionally duplicating data for performance optimization in specific use cases
SQL syntax — writing queries using SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, and JOIN clauses
Indexing strategies — creating indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, or ORDER BY clauses to improve query performance

knowledge.what_it_is: Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS) are database management systems that organize data into one or more tables with predefined schemas, using a structured query language to manage and manipulate data. PostgreSQL is an open-source RDBMS.
what_you_will_learn: ACID compliance — Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability: ensuring database transactions are processed reliably
Schema-on-write vs schema-on-read — understanding the trade-offs between defining table structures before or after data insertion
Normalization and denormalization — techniques for optimizing data storage and retrieval efficiency
Indexing strategies — B-tree, hash, GiST, SP-GiST: choosing the right indexing method for specific use cases
Locking mechanisms — row-level locking, table-level locking, advisory locks: managing concurrent access to resources
Query optimization techniques — index-only scans, parallel query execution, join order optimization: improving database performance

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL is an open-source, relational database management system (RDBMS) that supports a wide range of data types and offers advanced features like ACID compliance, transactions, and concurrency control. It is often compared to NoSQL databases due to its ability to handle large amounts of unstructured or semi-structured data.
what_you_will_learn: Relational vs NoSQL database models — understanding the differences in data modeling and querying
ACID compliance — PostgreSQL's guarantee of atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability for transactions
Data types in PostgreSQL — understanding the various data types supported by PostgreSQL, including JSON, XML, and arrays
Indexing strategies in PostgreSQL — learning about B-tree, hash, and GiST indexing techniques to improve query performance
PostgreSQL's concurrency control mechanisms — understanding how PostgreSQL manages concurrent transactions and locks
NoSQL database alternatives for PostgreSQL — exploring popular NoSQL databases like MongoDB, Cassandra, and Couchbase

knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, a table is a relational database object that stores data in rows and columns, similar to an Excel spreadsheet or a CSV file.
what_you_will_learn: Table creation — creating new tables using CREATE TABLE statement with column definitions and constraints
Table structure — understanding the concept of schema, including table name, columns, data types, and relationships between tables
Primary keys — defining primary keys to uniquely identify each row in a table, including auto-incrementing IDs and composite keys
Foreign keys — establishing relationships between tables using foreign key constraints, including cascade delete and update options
Table inheritance — creating child tables that inherit the structure of parent tables, with support for polymorphic queries
Partitioning — dividing large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces based on criteria such as date or value ranges
Clustering — reordering rows in a table to improve query performance by grouping related data together

knowledge.what_it_is: A schema in PostgreSQL is a collection of database objects, including tables, views, indexes, sequences, and functions, that define the structure and organization of data within a database.
what_you_will_learn: Table creation — creating new tables with specific column names, data types, and constraints using CREATE TABLE syntax
Column definitions — defining table columns with specific data types, nullability, and default values using COLUMN keyword
Primary key constraints — creating primary keys to uniquely identify rows within a table using PRIMARY KEY constraint
Foreign key constraints — establishing relationships between tables using FOREIGN KEY constraint
Index creation — creating indexes on specific columns or expressions to improve query performance using CREATE INDEX syntax
View creation — defining views as virtual tables based on the result of a SELECT statement using CREATE VIEW syntax
Sequence creation — creating sequences to generate unique values for identity columns using CREATE SEQUENCE syntax
Function creation — defining functions that perform specific actions or calculations using CREATE FUNCTION syntax

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL is an open-source, object-relational database management system (ORDBMS) that supports both relational and non-relational data models. It is widely used in enterprise environments due to its reliability, scalability, and high-performance capabilities.
what_you_will_learn: ACID compliance — understanding the Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability properties of PostgreSQL transactions
SQL vs NoSQL databases — comparing relational database management systems (RDBMS) like PostgreSQL with non-relational databases like MongoDB and Cassandra
PostgreSQL's data types — learning about the various data types supported by PostgreSQL, including numeric, character, date/time, and binary types
Indexing strategies in PostgreSQL — understanding how to optimize query performance using indexes, including B-tree, GiST, and SP-GiST indexing
PostgreSQL's concurrency control mechanisms — learning about the different methods used to manage concurrent access to data, such as locking and snapshot isolation
Data partitioning in PostgreSQL — understanding how to split large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces for improved performance and scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL is an open-source, object-relational database management system (ORDBMS) developed by the PostgreSQL Global Development Group. It supports a wide range of data types, including integers, strings, dates, and arrays, and provides advanced features such as transactions, views, and stored procedures.
what_you_will_learn: Data Types — understanding built-in data types like integer, string, date, time, timestamp, and array
Tables — creating and managing tables with columns, indexes, and constraints
Schemas — organizing database objects into logical groups using schemas
Views — defining virtual tables based on queries to simplify complex data access
Stored Procedures — writing reusable blocks of SQL code to perform tasks like data validation and manipulation
Transactions — understanding how PostgreSQL manages atomicity and consistency through transactions
Indexing — creating indexes to improve query performance, including B-tree, hash, and GiST indexes

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL is an open-source relational database management system (RDBMS) developed by PostgreSQL Global Development Group, known for its reliability, data integrity, and scalability.
what_you_will_learn: Data Types — understanding PostgreSQL's built-in data types such as integer, varchar, timestamp, and date
Table Structure — designing tables with columns, data types, and constraints like primary keys, foreign keys, and indexes
SQL Syntax — writing basic SQL queries using SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE statements with clauses like WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING
Database Schema — creating and managing database schema using CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE, DROP TABLE statements
Indexing — understanding how PostgreSQL uses indexes to improve query performance, including B-tree, hash, GiST, and SP-GiST index types
Transactions — working with transactions to ensure data consistency and isolation using COMMIT, ROLLBACK statements
Views — creating and managing views as virtual tables based on SELECT statements with optional WHERE, GROUP BY clauses

knowledge.what_it_is: Normalization is the process of organizing data in a database to minimize data redundancy and dependency, ensuring data consistency and improving scalability. In PostgreSQL, normalization is achieved through the application of normal forms.
what_you_will_learn: First Normal Form (1NF) — eliminating repeating groups by creating separate tables for each group
Second Normal Form (2NF) — removing partial dependencies by ensuring that non-key attributes depend on the entire primary key
Third Normal Form (3NF) — addressing transitive dependencies by separating related data into separate tables
Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF) — a stronger version of 3NF, eliminating functional dependencies between composite keys and non-key attributes
Fourth Normal Form (4NF) — handling multi-level dependencies by separating related data into separate tables
Domain-Key Normal Form (DKNF) — ensuring that each table has a primary key and all other columns depend on this key or are functionally dependent on the key
Denormalization in PostgreSQL — understanding when to denormalize for performance optimization, including techniques like materialized views and data warehousing

knowledge.what_it_is: The PostgreSQL object model defines how data is stored and organized within a database, including tables, relationships between them, indexes, views, and other schema objects.
what_you_will_learn: Tables — defining relational structures with columns, data types, and constraints
Schemas — organizing related tables into logical groups for better management and security
Relationships — establishing links between tables using foreign keys, primary keys, and join conditions
Indexes — optimizing query performance by creating efficient access paths to table data
Views — virtual tables that simplify complex queries or provide a layer of abstraction over underlying data
Sequences — generating unique identifiers for rows in a table, such as auto-incrementing IDs
Triggers — executing custom actions automatically when certain events occur within the database
Constraints — enforcing data integrity rules, like primary key uniqueness and foreign key relationships

knowledge.what_it_is: ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is a set of principles that ensures database transactions are processed reliably and securely. In PostgreSQL, ACID compliance is achieved through a combination of locking mechanisms, logging, and transaction management.
what_you_will_learn: Atomicity — Ensuring all operations within a transaction are executed as a single, indivisible unit (e.g., using SAVEPOINT to roll back partial transactions)
Consistency — Maintaining database consistency by enforcing referential integrity and constraints (e.g., using CHECK constraints and NOT NULL constraints)
Isolation — Preventing concurrent transactions from interfering with each other's results (e.g., using SERIALIZABLE isolation level and NOWAIT locking mode)
Durability — Ensuring once a transaction is committed, its effects are permanent and cannot be rolled back (e.g., using WAL logging and checkpointing)
Transaction Isolation Levels — Understanding the different isolation levels in PostgreSQL (READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE) and when to use each
Lock Modes — Familiarizing yourself with the different lock modes in PostgreSQL (SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE, SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, etc.) and how they impact concurrency
Transaction Management — Understanding how to manage transactions in PostgreSQL using commands like BEGIN, COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT, and RELEASE SAVEPOINT

knowledge.what_it_is: Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC) is a mechanism used by PostgreSQL to manage concurrency and ensure data consistency, allowing multiple transactions to read and write data simultaneously without locking the entire database.
what_you_will_learn: Lock modes — understanding the different types of locks in PostgreSQL: Share Locks, Exclusive Locks, Access Exclusive Locks
Transaction isolation levels — configuring transaction isolation levels (Read Committed, Read Uncommitted, Repeatable Read, Serializable) to control concurrency and consistency
Snapshot creation — how PostgreSQL creates snapshots of data at the start of each transaction to ensure consistent reads
Tuple visibility — understanding how PostgreSQL determines which tuples are visible to a transaction based on its snapshot
Deadlock detection and resolution — strategies for detecting and resolving deadlocks in MVCC, including using pg_stat_activity and pg_locks views
MVCC performance optimization — techniques for optimizing MVCC performance, such as indexing and partitioning
Handling concurrent updates — strategies for handling concurrent updates to the same row or table in MVCC

knowledge.what_it_is: A transaction in PostgreSQL is a sequence of operations performed as a single, all-or-nothing unit of work. It allows multiple statements to be executed together, ensuring data consistency and integrity.
what_you_will_learn: Transaction isolation levels — SERIALIZABLE, REPEATABLE READ, READ COMMITTED, READ UNCOMMITTED
Lock modes — EXCLUSIVE, SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE, SHARE
Transaction concurrency control — using LOCK TABLE FOR UPDATE and NOWAIT/NOROWWAIT
Savepoints — creating named savepoints for partial rollback and error handling
Nested transactions — executing multiple transactions within a single outer transaction
Transaction logging — understanding the PostgreSQL transaction log and its role in recovery
Distributed transactions — using PostgreSQL's built-in support for distributed transactions with other databases

knowledge.what_it_is: The Write-Ahead Log (WAL) is a mechanism used by PostgreSQL to record all changes made to the database, allowing for crash recovery and replication.
what_you_will_learn: WAL architecture — understanding how WAL records are generated, stored, and replayed
Checkpointing — configuring checkpoint intervals and settings to balance performance and durability
WalWriter process — learning about the background process responsible for writing WAL records to disk
wal_level parameter — configuring the level of logging detail (minimal, replica, logical, or physical)
WAL file management — understanding how PostgreSQL manages WAL files, including naming conventions and retention policies
Recovery Target Time (RTT) — configuring RTT to control the maximum time spent on crash recovery
Streaming replication — learning about the process of streaming WAL records from a primary server to standby servers

knowledge.what_it_is: Query processing is the process by which PostgreSQL executes SQL queries, transforming them into efficient execution plans that access and manipulate data stored on disk.
what_you_will_learn: Query planning — understanding how PostgreSQL's cost-based optimizer generates optimal query execution plans
Execution trees — learning about the internal representation of query execution plans in PostgreSQL
Join order optimization — techniques for optimizing join operations, including left-deep and right-deep join orders
Sort and group optimization — strategies for minimizing sort and group operations, such as using index scans or sorting in memory
Caching and materialization — understanding how PostgreSQL's query cache and materialized views can improve performance
Bitmap indexing — learning about the use of bitmap indexes to speed up range queries and joins
Index-only scans — techniques for minimizing disk I/O by accessing only indexed columns
Parallel query execution — strategies for executing queries in parallel, including using multiple CPU cores or distributed architectures

knowledge.what_it_is: Online Transactional Processing (OLTP) is a database management approach that focuses on high-performance transaction processing, typically used in applications with frequent updates and inserts. In PostgreSQL, OLTP is optimized through various features such as indexing, caching, and concurrent query execution.
what_you_will_learn: Row-level locking — PostgreSQL's default locking mechanism for ensuring data consistency during concurrent transactions
Tablespaces — organizing tables into separate storage locations to improve performance and manageability
Indexing strategies — understanding when to use B-tree, GiST, SP-GiST, and GIN indexes for efficient query execution
Caching mechanisms — PostgreSQL's built-in caching features, including shared buffers and the buffer cache
Concurrent query execution — techniques for optimizing concurrent queries, such as using parallel query and query cancellation
Transaction isolation levels — understanding the differences between Read Committed, Read Uncommitted, Repeatable Read, and Serializable
Lock modes — PostgreSQL's locking mechanisms, including exclusive locks, shared locks, and advisory locks

knowledge.what_it_is: OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) is a database technology that enables fast and efficient querying of large datasets, often used for business intelligence and data analytics. In PostgreSQL, OLAP capabilities are achieved through various extensions and tools.
what_you_will_learn: Star Schemas — designing star schema patterns to optimize query performance and reduce data redundancy
Cube Schemas — creating cube schemas to support advanced analytical queries and aggregations
Materialized Views — using materialized views to cache frequently queried results and improve query performance
OLAP Extensions (e.g. pg_cuboid, pg_mview) — leveraging PostgreSQL's built-in OLAP extensions for efficient data aggregation and analysis
Distributed Querying — understanding how PostgreSQL's distributed querying capabilities enable scalable OLAP workloads
Data Warehousing — designing and implementing data warehouses using PostgreSQL to support business intelligence and analytics

knowledge.what_it_is: HTAP (Hybrid Transactional/Analytical Processing) is an architecture that combines the strengths of OLTP (Online Transactional Processing) and OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) systems, enabling real-time analytics on operational data. In PostgreSQL, HTAP is achieved through the use of materialized views, which cache frequently accessed query results.
what_you_will_learn: Materialized Views — pre-computed result sets that speed up complex queries and enable real-time analytics
Incremental Refresh — automatic updating of materialized views using change tracking and delta storage
CTE (Common Table Expressions) — temporary result sets used to simplify complex queries and improve performance
Window Functions — aggregating data over a specified window or set of rows, useful for real-time analytics
PostgreSQL's built-in support for HTAP through the use of materialized views and incremental refresh

knowledge.what_it_is: A domain is a collection of related attributes, serving as a fundamental building block in relational databases like PostgreSQL. It represents a real-world concept or entity and provides a way to organize data into meaningful categories.
what_you_will_learn: Domain constraints — defining rules for valid values within a domain, such as check constraints
Domain inheritance — creating a new domain based on an existing one, inheriting its properties and constraints
Domain casting — converting between domains of different types, ensuring data integrity during operations
Domain-specific datatypes — using PostgreSQL's built-in datatypes like ENUM, INTERVAL, or MONEY to represent specific domain values
Domain-based normalization — organizing tables around domains to reduce data redundancy and improve scalability

knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, attributes are columns within a table that store data. They can be thought of as fields or properties of an entity. Attributes provide a way to organize and structure data within a database.
what_you_will_learn: Data Types — understanding the various data types supported by PostgreSQL, including integer, string, date, and more
Column Definitions — learning how to define columns with specific attributes such as NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY, and UNIQUE constraints
Attribute Naming Conventions — understanding the best practices for naming attributes in a database, including using singular nouns and camelCase notation
Data Type Modifiers — learning about data type modifiers such as precision and scale for numeric types
Column Constraints — understanding how to apply constraints to columns, including CHECK, DEFAULT, and FOREIGN KEY constraints
Attribute Data Types and Storage — understanding the impact of attribute data types on storage requirements and performance
Indexing Attributes — learning when and how to create indexes on attributes to improve query performance

knowledge.what_it_is: A tuple in PostgreSQL is an ordered collection of values that can be used to store and manipulate data. It is similar to a row in a table, but it does not have a schema associated with it.
what_you_will_learn: Creating tuples using the ROW() function — creating a new tuple from individual values
Accessing tuple elements using array indexing — accessing specific elements within a tuple
Using the ALL keyword to compare tuples — comparing two or more tuples for equality
Working with tuple types — defining and using custom tuple types in PostgreSQL
Converting between tuples and arrays — converting between these data structures in PostgreSQL
Using the ANY() function with tuples — performing operations on each element of a tuple
Creating and manipulating composite types — creating complex data structures using tuples

knowledge.what_it_is: In relational databases like PostgreSQL, relations represent tables that are connected through foreign keys, enabling complex data relationships and querying.
what_you_will_learn: Entity-relationship modeling — designing relations between tables to capture real-world entities and their interactions
Foreign key constraints — defining relationships between tables using primary and foreign keys
Join operations — combining rows from multiple tables based on common columns, including INNER JOIN, LEFT JOIN, RIGHT JOIN, FULL OUTER JOIN
Subqueries in joins — using subqueries to filter or aggregate data within a join operation
Table inheritance — creating parent-child relationships between tables for efficient data storage and querying
Materialized views — storing pre-computed results of complex queries as physical tables for faster query performance
Common table expressions (CTEs) — defining temporary result sets that can be referenced within a query, useful for recursive queries or complex calculations

knowledge.what_it_is: Constraints are rules that enforce data integrity and consistency within PostgreSQL databases, ensuring data accuracy and reliability.
what_you_will_learn: Primary Keys — uniquely identifying each row in a table using a single column or composite key
Foreign Keys — referencing the primary key of another table to establish relationships between tables
Unique Constraints — preventing duplicate values within a column or set of columns
Check Constraints — validating data based on conditions or rules, such as minimum/maximum values or specific formats
Exclusion Constraints — ensuring that rows in one table do not overlap with rows in another table
Deferrable Constraints — allowing constraints to be checked after the current transaction is committed
Constraint Indexes — creating indexes on columns used by constraints for efficient constraint checking

knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, NULL represents an unknown or missing value in a column, distinct from zero or empty strings.
what_you_will_learn: Nullability — understanding how to declare columns as nullable or not nullable using NOT NULL constraint
NULL vs. EMPTY STRING — distinguishing between NULL and empty string values in PostgreSQL
COALESCE() function — replacing NULL with a default value, handling missing data
IS NULL operator — checking for NULL values in queries, filtering out unknowns
IS NOT NULL operator — excluding rows with NULL values from query results
NULLIF() function — returning NULL if both arguments match, useful for conditional logic
NVL() function (PostgreSQL alias) — replacing NULL with a default value, equivalent to COALESCE()
Handling NULL in aggregate functions — how PostgreSQL handles NULL values in SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN

knowledge.what_it_is: Schemas are logical containers that define the structure of data within a PostgreSQL database, including tables, views, sequences, and indexes.
what_you_will_learn: Creating schemas using SQL commands: `CREATE SCHEMA` — creates a new schema, `ALTER SCHEMA` — renames or alters an existing schema
Granting privileges on schemas: `GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA myschema TO myuser;` — grants all privileges to a user on a schema
Schema search path: `$psql -c 'SHOW search_path;'` — displays the current schema search path, `ALTER ROLE myrole SET search_path = 'myschema';` — sets the schema search path for a role
Schema binding: `CREATE TABLE mytable (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY) INHERITS (myschema.mytable);` — creates a table that inherits from another table in the same schema

Schema inheritance: `CREATE TABLE mychild (CHECK (age > 18));` — creates a child table with its own constraints, `ALTER TABLE myparent ADD CONSTRAINT check_age CHECK (age > 18);` — adds a constraint to a parent table

knowledge.what_it_is: Docker is a containerization platform that allows developers to package, ship, and run any application as a lightweight and portable container, ensuring consistency across different environments.
what_you_will_learn: Containerization — packaging applications with their dependencies into containers for consistent deployment
Images — read-only templates used to create containers, similar to virtual machines but much lighter
Containers — runtime instances of images, providing a isolated environment for the application and its dependencies
Volumes — directories mounted from the host machine to persist data even after container restart or deletion
Networking — Docker's networking model allows containers to communicate with each other and external services using IP addresses and ports
Docker Compose — a tool for defining and running multi-container applications, simplifying development and testing workflows
Docker Hub — a cloud-based registry for sharing and discovering Docker images, including official PostgreSQL images
Container orchestration — managing multiple containers as a single unit using tools like Kubernetes or Docker Swarm

knowledge.what_it_is: Foreign tables are PostgreSQL's way of referencing data from external sources, such as other databases or files, without actually copying it into the local database. This allows for efficient querying and manipulation of remote data.
what_you_will_learn: Foreign table creation — creating a foreign table using the CREATE FOREIGN TABLE statement
Foreign key constraints — defining foreign key relationships between tables in the same database or across databases
External tables — referencing external data sources, such as files or other databases, using the CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE statement
Table inheritance — using inheritance to create a new table that inherits columns and properties from an existing table
Partitioning — dividing large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces based on a partitioning key
Materialized views — storing the result of a query in a physical table for faster access
Common table expressions (CTEs) — defining temporary result sets that can be referenced within a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement

knowledge.what_it_is: systemd is a system and service manager for Linux operating systems, responsible for managing system services, including PostgreSQL, during boot and runtime.
what_you_will_learn: Systemd units — defining and managing PostgreSQL-related systemd units (services, sockets, timers)
Service file configuration — customizing PostgreSQL service files to manage startup, shutdown, and restart
Target units — using target units to manage PostgreSQL services during system boot and runtime
Socket activation — enabling PostgreSQL to listen for incoming connections via socket activation
Timer units — scheduling PostgreSQL maintenance tasks (e.g., backups) with systemd timer units
Systemd journal logging — configuring PostgreSQL to log events to the systemd journal
Service status management — using systemctl to manage PostgreSQL service status and restarts

knowledge.what_it_is: A package manager for PostgreSQL is a tool that allows you to easily install, update, and manage packages (libraries) written in various programming languages. It provides a convenient way to extend the functionality of your PostgreSQL database without modifying its core code.
what_you_will_learn: pgxn — The PostgreSQL Extension Network: a package manager for PostgreSQL extensions
pg_config — A tool that generates Makefiles and other build files for PostgreSQL packages
pg_buildpackage — A tool that builds PostgreSQL packages from source
pgXS — A way to distribute PostgreSQL extensions as separate packages
PGPICK — A package manager for PostgreSQL that allows you to easily install, update, and manage packages
pg_repack — A tool that repacks PostgreSQL databases, including their associated files, into a single file
pg_dumpall — A command-line utility that dumps the entire database, including its structure and data, into a SQL script
pg_restore — A command-line utility that restores a PostgreSQL database from a backup file

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_ctl is a command-line utility used to manage and control PostgreSQL database clusters, including starting, stopping, and restarting them.
what_you_will_learn: Starting and stopping PostgreSQL clusters — pg_ctl start and pg_ctl stop commands
Checking the status of a PostgreSQL cluster — pg_ctl status command
Restarting a PostgreSQL cluster — pg_ctl restart command
Setting environment variables for PostgreSQL — PGDATA, PGUSER, PGPASSWORD, PGHOST, and PGPORT
Configuring PostgreSQL cluster settings using pg_ctl config command
Managing PostgreSQL cluster logs using pg_ctl log command
Using pg_ctl with systemd to manage PostgreSQL services on Linux systems

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_ctlcluster is a command-line utility used to manage PostgreSQL clusters, including starting, stopping, and restarting them.
what_you_will_learn: Cluster management — managing multiple PostgreSQL instances as a single cluster
Cluster startup and shutdown scripts — creating custom scripts for automating cluster start/stop processes
pg_ctlcluster command options — understanding available flags for controlling cluster behavior (e.g., -m, -t)
Cluster status monitoring — using pg_ctlcluster to check cluster health and identify potential issues
Autostart configuration — configuring PostgreSQL to automatically start the cluster on system boot
PostgreSQL service management — managing PostgreSQL services through pg_ctlcluster
Cluster backup and restore — using pg_ctlcluster to manage backups and restores of entire clusters
subtopics: pg_ctlcluster flags for managing cluster behavior
cluster startup and shutdown script examples

knowledge.what_it_is: Connecting to PostgreSQL databases using the command-line tool `psql` allows you to interact with your database, execute SQL queries, and manage database objects. It is a crucial skill for any PostgreSQL developer or administrator.
what_you_will_learn: Connecting to a PostgreSQL database — specifying host, port, username, and password
Basic SQL commands in `psql` — SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, DROP, ALTER
Database navigation — listing databases, switching between databases, creating new databases
Table management — creating tables, dropping tables, altering table structures
Querying data — using SQL queries to retrieve and manipulate data in tables
Error handling — understanding and fixing common errors encountered while connecting or executing queries
Configuring `psql` settings — customizing prompt, output format, and other behavior

knowledge.what_it_is: Cloud deployment for PostgreSQL involves setting up and managing PostgreSQL databases on cloud platforms such as Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, or Azure Database Services. This allows for scalable, highly available, and secure database management.
what_you_will_learn: Creating a PostgreSQL instance on AWS RDS — configuring instance type, storage, and security settings
Setting up PostgreSQL on Google Cloud SQL — managing instances, databases, and users through the Cloud Console
Deploying PostgreSQL on Azure Database Services — creating servers, databases, and firewall rules using the Azure portal or CLI
Using PostgreSQL with Amazon Aurora — migrating to a high-performance, MySQL-compatible database service
Configuring PostgreSQL for high availability on Google Cloud SQL — setting up regional replicas and failover policies
Managing PostgreSQL backups and snapshots on cloud platforms — scheduling automatic backups and restoring from snapshots

knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, data types define the structure and format of data stored in tables. They determine how values are interpreted and used in queries.
what_you_will_learn: Integer — a whole number type that can store integers between -2^31 and 2^31-1
Smallint — a smaller integer type that can store integers between -2^15 and 2^15-1
Bigint — a larger integer type that can store integers between -2^63 and 2^63-1
Real — a floating-point number type with single precision, suitable for most applications
Double Precision — a floating-point number type with double precision, suitable for high-precision calculations
Character Varying (VARCHAR) — a string type that can store variable-length character strings
Text — a string type that can store fixed-length or variable-length character strings
Boolean — a logical data type that can store true or false values
Date — a date type that can store dates in the format YYYY-MM-DD
Time — a time type that can store times in the format HH24:MI:SS
Timestamp — a timestamp type that can store both date and time information

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL provides various ways to modify data, including updating existing rows, inserting new rows, and deleting rows.
what_you_will_learn: UPDATE — modifying existing rows using a SELECT-like syntax with WHERE clause for conditional updates
INSERT INTO — adding new rows to a table, specifying column names and values
DELETE FROM — removing rows from a table, optionally using a WHERE clause for conditional deletion
MERGE INTO — combining data from two tables into one, similar to an UPDATE or INSERT operation
UPSERT — updating existing rows or inserting new ones based on a unique constraint or index
TRUNCATE TABLE — quickly deleting all rows from a table, often used in development or testing environments
ROW LEVEL SECURITY (RLS) — controlling access to data at the row level using policies and roles
EXCLUSIVE LOCK — acquiring an exclusive lock on a row or table for concurrent modification

knowledge.what_it_is: The COPY command in PostgreSQL allows you to import and export data from a file into or out of a database table. It's a powerful tool for bulk loading and unloading data, often used in ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) processes.
what_you_will_learn: COPY FROM — importing data from a file into a PostgreSQL table using various formats like CSV, JSON, or XML
COPY TO — exporting data from a PostgreSQL table to a file using various formats like CSV, JSON, or XML
Format options — understanding the different format options for COPY FROM and COPY TO, such as DELIMITER, HEADER, QUOTE, and ESCAPE
File handling — learning how to handle files in PostgreSQL, including specifying file paths, permissions, and encoding
Performance optimization — techniques for optimizing COPY performance, such as parallel processing, compression, and buffering
Error handling — understanding how to handle errors during the import/export process, including error messages and logging
Security considerations — best practices for securing data imports and exports, including access control and encryption

knowledge.what_it_is: Querying data in PostgreSQL involves using SQL commands to retrieve and manipulate data stored in tables.
what_you_will_learn: SELECT statements — retrieving specific columns from a table, filtering with WHERE clauses
FROM clause — specifying the table(s) to query, joining multiple tables with JOINs
WHERE clause — filtering rows based on conditions, using operators like =, <, >, LIKE, IS NULL
ORDER BY clause — sorting result sets in ascending or descending order by one or more columns
LIMIT and OFFSET clauses — paginating results to limit the number of rows returned
EXISTS and IN operators — checking for existence of rows in a subquery or another table
NOT EXISTS, NOT IN, and NOT ILIKE operators — negating the results of EXISTS, IN, and ILIKE operations
DISTINCT keyword — removing duplicate rows from result sets
GROUP BY clause — aggregating data by one or more columns, using functions like SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN

knowledge.what_it_is: Data filtering in PostgreSQL allows you to select specific rows from a table based on conditions, reducing data retrieval and processing overhead.
what_you_will_learn: WHERE clause — specifying conditions for row selection using logical operators (AND, OR, NOT), comparison operators (=, <>, LIKE, ILIKE), and functions (NOW(), CURRENT_DATE)
FILTER clause — filtering rows in window functions (e.g., ROW_NUMBER(), RANK())
EXISTS and NOT EXISTS clauses — testing the existence of rows in a subquery or joined table
IN and NOT IN operators — selecting rows based on membership in a set or array
ANY and ALL operators — applying conditions to multiple values using aggregate functions (e.g., SUM(), AVG())
DISTINCT keyword — removing duplicate rows from the result set
ORDER BY clause with LIMIT and OFFSET — filtering results by sorting and limiting the number of rows returned

knowledge.what_it_is: Table joining is a fundamental concept in SQL that allows you to combine rows from two or more tables based on a common column, enabling complex data analysis and manipulation.
what_you_will_learn: Inner joins — combining rows where there are matching values in both tables using the INNER keyword
Left outer joins — returning all records from the left table and matching records from the right table using the LEFT OUTER JOIN clause
Right outer joins — similar to left outer joins but returns all records from the right table and matching records from the left table
Full outer joins — combining all rows from both tables, including NULL values where there are no matches
Cross joins — returning the Cartesian product of rows from two or more tables using the CROSS JOIN clause
Using indexes for efficient joining — leveraging PostgreSQL's indexing capabilities to speed up join operations
Joining multiple tables at once — combining three or more tables in a single query using the INNER, LEFT OUTER, RIGHT OUTER, and FULL OUTER keywords

knowledge.what_it_is: Common Table Expressions (CTEs) are temporary result sets that can be referenced within a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement. They allow you to simplify complex queries and improve readability.
what_you_will_learn: Defining CTEs using the WITH keyword — a temporary table can be defined with a name and used in the main query
Using recursive CTEs for hierarchical data — traversing tree-like structures or graph data
CTE caching — improving performance by reusing previously computed results
Materialized CTEs — storing intermediate results in disk to speed up subsequent queries
Subquery vs. CTE: when to use each and how they differ
Using CTEs with window functions for more complex aggregations

knowledge.what_it_is: A lateral join in PostgreSQL allows you to perform joins with tables that are dependent on the current row of another table, often used with arrays or JSON data.
what_you_will_learn: Lateral join syntax — writing a lateral join query using the LATERAL keyword
Table-valued functions (TVFs) — creating and using TVFs to perform complex calculations in joins
Array and JSON processing — using PostgreSQL's array and JSON functions with lateral joins for efficient data manipulation
Correlated subqueries vs. lateral joins — understanding when to use each approach for joining tables
Lateral join optimization — strategies for improving the performance of lateral join queries, including indexing and rewriting queries
Using LATERAL with Common Table Expressions (CTEs) — combining lateral joins with CTEs for more complex data transformations

knowledge.what_it_is: A subquery is a query nested inside another query, used to perform complex data retrieval and manipulation operations.
what_you_will_learn: Correlated subqueries — using variables from the outer query to filter or aggregate data in the inner query
Non-correlated subqueries — retrieving data independently of the outer query's conditions
EXISTS clause — checking if a subquery returns any rows, used for existence checks and filtering
IN operator — using a subquery as an argument to the IN operator for value-based filtering
ALL/ANY/SOME operators — applying aggregate functions to subqueries for conditional logic
LATERAL joins with subqueries — combining lateral joins with subqueries for complex data retrieval
Common Table Expressions (CTEs) with subqueries — using CTEs as intermediate results in subqueries

knowledge.what_it_is: Set operations are used to combine or manipulate sets of rows in PostgreSQL, allowing you to perform complex queries and data analysis.
what_you_will_learn: UNION — combining two or more result sets into a single set, removing duplicates
INTERSECT — returning only the rows that are common to both result sets
EXCEPT — returning all rows from one result set that are not present in another
SET OPERATOR syntax — using UNION ALL, INTERSECT ALL, and EXCEPT ALL for more control over query results
DISTINCT ON — removing duplicates based on a specific column or expression
ROW_NUMBER() and RANK() functions — assigning a unique number to each row within a partition of a result set
Common Table Expressions (CTEs) with SET OPERATIONS — using CTEs to simplify complex queries involving set operations

knowledge.what_it_is: Grouping is a SQL feature that allows you to perform aggregation operations on multiple columns simultaneously, enabling efficient data summarization and analysis.
what_you_will_learn: GROUP BY — specifying one or more columns to group rows by
ROLLUP — generating subtotals for each grouping level
CUBE — generating all possible combinations of subtotals for each grouping level
GROUPING SETS — specifying multiple grouping sets in a single query
GROUPING ID — assigning a unique identifier to each grouping set
AGGREGATE functions — using built-in aggregate functions like SUM, AVG, MAX, MIN with GROUP BY
WINDOW functions — applying window functions like ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK with GROUP BY

knowledge.what_it_is: Aggregate and window functions are used to perform calculations on sets of rows that meet specific conditions, often used with GROUP BY clauses.
what_you_will_learn: GROUP BY — grouping rows by one or more columns, followed by an aggregate function (e.g., SUM, AVG, COUNT)
ROLLUP and CUBE — generating multiple groupings from a single query
WINDOW functions (e.g., ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK()) — assigning a ranking to each row within a partition of a result set
LAG and LEAD — accessing data from previous or next rows in the result set
NTILE() — dividing an ordered dataset into a specified number of groups
PERCENT_RANK() and CUME_DIST() — calculating percent rank and cumulative distribution for each row within a partition
ROWS and RANGE frames — specifying which rows to include in window function calculations (e.g., current row, preceding/preceding rows)
PARTITION BY and ORDER BY clauses — dividing the result set into partitions based on one or more columns and sorting data within each partition

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL's built-in reporting, logging, and statistics features enable database administrators to monitor performance, troubleshoot issues, and optimize queries. These tools are essential for maintaining a high-performing PostgreSQL instance.
what_you_will_learn: pg_stat_statements — monitoring query execution plans and statistics
pg_stat_activity — tracking current connections, sessions, and query details
pg_stat_database — database-level performance metrics, including queries, transactions, and locks
pg_stat_user_functions — function-level performance metrics, including execution counts and times
pg_stat_user_tables — table-level performance metrics, including reads, writes, and vacuum operations
pg_log — PostgreSQL's built-in logging system for monitoring database events and errors
pg_stat_reset — resetting statistics to their initial state for accurate tracking
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE) — visualizing query execution plans with detailed timing information

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL extensions are shared libraries that can add new functionality to a database, such as data types, operators, and functions.
what_you_will_learn: Extension installation — installing extensions using CREATE EXTENSION or pg_extension module
Extension management — listing, dropping, and upgrading extensions using SQL commands
pg_dump and pg_restore with extensions — including extensions in backups and restores
PostGIS extension — adding support for geospatial data types and functions
PL/pgSQL procedural language — creating stored procedures and functions using PL/pgSQL
Perl and Python procedural languages — creating stored procedures and functions using Perl and Python
Custom data types — creating custom data types using CREATE TYPE command
Custom operators — creating custom operators using CREATE OPERATOR command

knowledge.what_it_is: Resource usage monitoring and optimization is crucial to ensure efficient database performance, scalability, and cost-effectiveness. PostgreSQL provides various tools and techniques to monitor and manage resource utilization.
what_you_will_learn: pg_stat_activity — monitoring active sessions, queries, and connections
pg_stat_database — tracking database-level metrics such as disk usage, connections, and transactions
pg_stat_user_functions — analyzing function call statistics for optimization
pg_stat_user_tables — monitoring table-level metrics like rows scanned, inserted, updated, and deleted
pg_stat_io — tracking I/O statistics for storage performance optimization
pg_stat_user_indexes — analyzing index usage and maintenance recommendations
Resource Queues (RQ) — managing resource allocation and prioritization for efficient query execution
pg_cancel_backend — canceling long-running queries to free resources

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL's advanced topics cover high-performance tuning, security best practices, and cutting-edge features like parallel query execution, vectorized operations, and window functions.
what_you_will_learn: Parallel Query Execution — enabling concurrent processing of multiple queries to improve performance on multi-core systems
Vectorized Operations — using SIMD instructions to accelerate arithmetic and logical operations on large datasets
Window Functions — performing calculations across rows that are logically related, such as aggregations and rankings
Materialized Views — storing the result of a query in a physical table for faster access and reduced computation
Common Table Expressions (CTEs) — defining temporary result sets that can be referenced within a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement
Partitioning — dividing large tables into smaller, more manageable pieces based on a specific column or set of columns
Index Hints — specifying the order in which indexes should be used to improve query performance and avoid index scans
Query Optimization — using tools like EXPLAIN ANALYZE and pg_stat_statements to identify bottlenecks and optimize queries

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL authentication models define how users and roles interact with the database, controlling access to resources and enforcing security policies.
what_you_will_learn: Peer Authentication — authenticating users based on their operating system credentials
Ident File Authentication — using a file to store user identities and passwords
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) Authentication — integrating with external directory services for centralized identity management
GSSAPI (Generic Security Service Application Program Interface) Authentication — using Kerberos or other GSSAPI-enabled systems for secure authentication
RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service) Authentication — using a RADIUS server to authenticate users and manage access
Certificate-Based Authentication — verifying user identities through digital certificates
PAM (Pluggable Authentication Module) Authentication — integrating with operating system PAM modules for flexible authentication
SQL Authentication — authenticating users based on their database credentials

knowledge.what_it_is: The pg_hba.conf file is a configuration file in PostgreSQL that controls remote access to databases, including authentication and authorization methods.
what_you_will_learn: Host-based access control — configuring rules for incoming connections based on IP addresses, hostnames, or network masks
Authentication methods — understanding the different types of authentication supported by pg_hba.conf, such as password, md5, scram-sha-256, and peer
Role mapping — defining how PostgreSQL roles are mapped to external systems or services for authentication and authorization
Database access control — configuring rules for accessing specific databases, tables, or columns within a database
Connection parameters — specifying connection settings such as encryption, compression, and idle timeout
pg_hba.conf file structure — understanding the format of the pg_hba.conf file, including sections, lines, and keywords
Rule precedence — determining how PostgreSQL resolves conflicts between multiple rules in the pg_hba.conf file

knowledge.what_it_is: In PostgreSQL, roles are used to manage user authentication and authorization. They can be thought of as a way to group users with similar privileges together.
what_you_will_learn: Role hierarchy — understanding how roles inherit permissions from parent roles
Role creation — creating new roles using CREATE ROLE or ALTER ROLE commands
Role membership — adding users to roles using GRANT or ADD ROLE commands
Privilege management — assigning privileges to roles using GRANT or REVOKE commands
Role inheritance — understanding how roles inherit permissions from parent roles and child roles
Role activation — activating or deactivating roles using ALTER ROLE command
Role renaming — renaming roles using ALTER ROLE command
Role dropping — dropping roles using DROP ROLE command

knowledge.what_it_is: SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux) is a mandatory access control (MAC) system that provides an additional layer of security to PostgreSQL by enforcing strict policies on user and process interactions. It is used to prevent unauthorized access, data breaches, and other security threats.
what_you_will_learn: SELinux modes — understanding the different SELinux modes: enforcing, permissive, and disabled
SELinux contexts — learning about the various SELinux contexts, including user, role, type, and level
PostgreSQL SELinux policies — configuring and managing PostgreSQL-specific SELinux policies using tools like semanage and setsebool
SELinux booleans — controlling access to PostgreSQL resources using SELinux booleans (e.g., allow_postgresql_connect)
SELinux file contexts — understanding how SELinux assigns file contexts to PostgreSQL files and directories
SELinux user mapping — configuring SELinux to map PostgreSQL users to system users
SELinux role mapping — configuring SELinux to map PostgreSQL roles to system roles

knowledge.what_it_is: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) settings in PostgreSQL enable secure connections between clients and servers, encrypting data transmitted over the network to prevent eavesdropping and tampering. This is crucial for protecting sensitive data, such as passwords and credit card numbers.
what_you_will_learn: pg_hba.conf — configuring SSL/TLS encryption for incoming connections using host-based access control
ssl_cert_file — specifying the path to the server's SSL certificate file
ssl_key_file — specifying the path to the server's SSL private key file
ssl_ca_file — specifying the path to the server's SSL Certificate Authority (CA) file
ssl_crl_file — specifying the path to the server's SSL Certificate Revocation List (CRL) file
SSL/TLS protocol versions — configuring supported protocol versions, such as TLSv1.2 and TLSv1.3
Cipher suites — selecting supported cipher suites for encryption, such as AES-256-GCM and ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA256

knowledge.what_it_is: Default privileges are the initial set of permissions granted to new objects (tables, views, sequences, etc.) created within a PostgreSQL database. They determine what actions users can perform on these objects by default.
what_you_will_learn: Granting vs. Revoking: understanding the difference between granting and revoking privileges in PostgreSQL
Default Privileges for Tables — automatically assigning SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE permissions to new tables created within a schema or database
Privilege Inheritance — how default privileges are inherited by child objects (e.g., views, sequences) from their parent objects
ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES — modifying the default privileges of existing objects using this command
pg_default_acl — understanding the system catalog that stores default ACLs for new objects created within a database
Default Privileges for Views — automatically assigning SELECT permission to new views created within a schema or database
Privilege Escalation Prevention — techniques to prevent users from escalating their privileges through default object permissions

knowledge.what_it_is: GRANT and REVOKE are SQL commands used to assign or revoke privileges on PostgreSQL database objects, such as tables, views, and sequences. This allows users to control access to sensitive data and ensure data integrity.
what_you_will_learn: Granting roles — assigning a role to a user or another role, allowing them to inherit all the privileges of that role
Revoke roles — removing a role from a user or another role, preventing them from accessing certain privileges
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON DATABASE — granting all possible privileges on a database to a user or role
REVOKE ALL PRIVILEGES ON SCHEMA — revoking all possible privileges on a schema from a user or role
Granting specific permissions — assigning individual permissions, such as SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE, on specific tables or views
Revoke specific permissions — removing individual permissions from users or roles
GRANT OPTION — allowing a user or role to grant privileges to others
WITH GRANT OPTION — specifying that the granted privilege can be further granted by the recipient
REVOKE OPTION — revoking the ability of a user or role to grant privileges

knowledge.what_it_is: Object privileges in PostgreSQL refer to the permissions granted to users or roles on database objects such as tables, views, sequences, and indexes. These privileges determine what actions can be performed on these objects, including SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, and more.
what_you_will_learn: Granting object privileges — using GRANT command with specific privileges (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) and specifying the object name
Revoke object privileges — using REVOKE command to remove previously granted privileges
Object privilege categories — understanding the different types of privileges such as SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, TRIGGER, EXECUTE, and others
Privilege inheritance — how privileges are inherited by child tables or views from their parent objects
Granting object privileges on specific columns — using GRANT command with column names to restrict access to sensitive data
Using pg_catalog.pg_class and pg_catalog.pg_namespace to manage object privileges programmatically
Understanding the role of security labels in PostgreSQL 13 and later versions for fine-grained access control

knowledge.what_it_is: Row-Level Security (RLS) is a PostgreSQL feature that allows fine-grained access control to rows within tables, enabling administrators to define complex security policies based on user attributes and table data.
what_you_will_learn: Policy Hierarchy — defining RLS policies as hierarchical structures with parent-child relationships
Row Security Policies — creating policies that restrict access to specific rows or sets of rows within a table
User Attributes — using PostgreSQL's built-in functions (e.g., current_user, session_user) and operators (e.g., =, <>) to define user attributes for RLS policy evaluation
Table Hints — specifying hints in SQL queries to override default RLS policies and allow access to restricted rows
Policy Evaluation Functions — using built-in functions (e.g., pg_get_policy, pg_has_role) to evaluate RLS policies and determine row-level access
Security Labels — assigning labels to tables or columns to enable more fine-grained control over data access
Label-Based Policies — creating policies that restrict access based on security labels assigned to rows or sets of rows

knowledge.what_it_is: PostgreSQL Anonymizer is a tool used to remove sensitive data from PostgreSQL databases, ensuring compliance with regulations such as GDPR and HIPAA. It is an essential skill for database administrators and security professionals.
what_you_will_learn: pg_anonymizer — the official PostgreSQL anonymization tool, capable of removing sensitive data from tables and views
anonymization techniques — understanding how to use pg_anonymizer's built-in methods (e.g., hashing, tokenization) or custom scripts for complex anonymization tasks
data classification — identifying sensitive columns and data types to be anonymized, using tools like pg_catalog.pg_get_userbyid()
anonymization strategies — learning when to use full anonymization vs. partial anonymization (e.g., masking credit card numbers)
pg_anonymizer configuration files — understanding how to customize the anonymization process through pg_anonymizer's XML-based config files
audit logging — setting up pg_anonymizer to log all anonymization activities, including what data was removed and when
data validation — ensuring that anonymized data is consistent with original data types and constraints

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_dump is a PostgreSQL utility that creates a portable, human-readable representation of your database schema and data, which can be used to create a backup or clone a database.
what_you_will_learn: Backup formats — pg_dump supports SQL (default), CSV, and custom formats like JSON and XML
Database connection options — specifying host, port, username, password, and other parameters using the -h, -p, -U, and -d flags
Schema and table selection — dumping specific schemas or tables using the -n and -t flags
Data types and encoding — handling character encodings like UTF-8 and custom data types with pg_dump's -E flag
SQL syntax options — controlling SQL syntax with the -b, -c, -e, and -x flags for compatibility and optimization
Backup validation — verifying the integrity of your backup using pg_verifybackup or pg_restore's --validate option
Customizing dump output — using the -f flag to specify a custom file name or directory for the dump

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_dumpall is a PostgreSQL utility that dumps all databases, including system catalogs and user-defined databases, into a single SQL script file.
what_you_will_learn: Database selection — specifying which databases to dump using the -d option
Dump options — controlling output format, compression, and other settings with command-line flags (e.g., -F, -b, -c)
System catalog dumping — understanding how pg_dumpall handles system catalogs and their dependencies
User-defined database dumping — learning how to dump user-defined databases, including schema and data
SQL script generation — understanding the output format of pg_dumpall, which can be customized with various options (e.g., -f)
Error handling — recognizing common errors and issues that may arise during the dumping process

knowledge.what_it_is: Barman is an open-source PostgreSQL backup and recovery manager, designed to simplify and automate database backups, archiving, and point-in-time recovery. It provides a flexible and scalable solution for managing large-scale PostgreSQL deployments.
what_you_will_learn: Backup configuration — defining backup settings, schedules, and retention policies
Backup types — understanding full, incremental, and differential backups in Barman
WAL archiving — configuring Barman to archive WAL files from PostgreSQL servers
Point-in-time recovery (PITR) — restoring a database to a specific point in time using Barman
Barman cluster setup — creating a Barman cluster for managing multiple PostgreSQL servers
Backup validation — verifying the integrity of backups and detecting potential issues
Recovery catalog — maintaining a centralized repository of backup metadata and recovery information

knowledge.what_it_is: WAL-G (Write Ahead Logging - Global) is a PostgreSQL tool for managing and replicating WAL files, providing a global view of WAL segments across multiple nodes. It's designed to simplify WAL management and ensure data consistency.
what_you_will_learn: Configuring WAL-G — setting up the wal-g directory, specifying the storage location, and configuring replication settings
wal-g init — initializing the wal-g configuration file, including database connection details and replication parameters
wal-g sync — synchronizing WAL files between nodes, ensuring data consistency across the cluster
wal-g backup — creating backups of WAL files, useful for disaster recovery and point-in-time recovery
wal-g restore — restoring WAL files from a backup, allowing for quick recovery in case of data loss
wal-g status — monitoring WAL-G status, including replication lag, storage usage, and other metrics
wal-g prune — removing unnecessary WAL files, optimizing storage space and improving performance

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_upgrade is a PostgreSQL utility that allows you to upgrade your database from one major version to another, while minimizing downtime and preserving data integrity. It's an essential tool for maintaining high availability and ensuring smooth upgrades.
what_you_will_learn: Upgrading from minor versions — pg_upgrade can handle minor version upgrades, but it's not recommended as it may lead to inconsistencies
Major version upgrades — understanding the process of upgrading from one major PostgreSQL version to another, including data consistency checks and potential issues with extensions
Backup and restore considerations — learning how to backup and restore your database before and after an upgrade using pg_dump, pg_dumpall, or other tools
pg_upgrade modes — familiarizing yourself with the different modes available in pg_upgrade, such as 'synchronous', 'async', and 'fast' upgrades
Postgres version compatibility — understanding which PostgreSQL versions are compatible for upgrading and potential issues with specific versions
Extension management — learning how to manage extensions during an upgrade, including potential issues with extension dependencies and conflicts
Upgrade logging and monitoring — configuring pg_upgrade to generate detailed logs and setting up monitoring tools to track the upgrade process

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_restore is a PostgreSQL utility that restores data from a backup file created by pg_dump or other tools, allowing you to recover your database's schema and data.
what_you_will_learn: Backup files — understanding the different types of backup files generated by pg_dump (plain text, custom format, tar)
Restore modes — learning about the different restore modes supported by pg_restore (single-transaction, extended logging, verbose output)
Database options — configuring database-specific settings during restore (e.g., owner, encoding, locale)
Tablespace management — restoring data to specific tablespaces for improved performance and storage efficiency
Error handling — understanding how pg_restore handles errors during the restore process and how to customize error behavior
Restore from compressed files — learning how to restore from compressed backup files using tools like gzip or bzip2
Incremental backups — restoring incremental backups generated by tools like WAL-G or Barman

knowledge.what_it_is: pgBackRest is an open-source, PostgreSQL-compatible backup and recovery solution that provides efficient, reliable, and customizable backups.
what_you_will_learn: Backup configurations — creating and managing pgBackRest configuration files to customize backup settings
Repository management — understanding how to create, manage, and maintain pgBackRest repositories for storing backups
Backup scheduling — using tools like cron or systemd timers to automate regular backups with pgBackRest
Incremental backups — learning how to perform incremental backups to reduce storage usage and improve recovery times
Point-in-time recovery (PITR) — understanding how to use pgBackRest's PITR feature for efficient point-in-time recovery of databases
Wal-E integration — integrating pgBackRest with Wal-E for automated WAL file management and backup
Backup validation — learning how to validate backups using pgBackRest's built-in validation tools

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_basebackup is a PostgreSQL utility for creating a consistent and recoverable backup of a PostgreSQL database cluster, including all data files, configuration files, and WAL files. It is designed to be used with PostgreSQL's built-in replication features.
what_you_will_learn: Backup modes — understanding the different backup modes available in pg_basebackup (streamed, tar, and compressed)
Backup options — configuring pg_basebackup options such as compression level, encryption, and archive mode
WAL file handling — managing WAL files during a backup, including archiving and compressing them
Backup validation — verifying the integrity of a backup using pg_verifybackup.pl or other tools
Restore procedures — understanding how to restore a database from a pg_basebackup backup, including restoring individual tables and databases
pg_basebackup configuration — configuring pg_basebackup settings in postgresql.conf and recovery.conf files
Backup retention policies — implementing backup retention policies using tools like pgbackrest or WAL-G

knowledge.what_it_is: pg_probackup is an open-source PostgreSQL backup and recovery tool, designed to provide efficient and reliable backups of PostgreSQL databases.
what_you_will_learn: Backup strategies — understanding the different types of backups (full, incremental, differential) and when to use each
Backup configuration files — creating and managing pg_probackup.conf files for customizing backup settings
Repository management — setting up and maintaining repositories for storing backed-up data
Backup schedules — automating regular backups using cron jobs or other scheduling tools
Restore procedures — understanding how to restore databases from backups, including partial restores and point-in-time recovery
Incremental backup processing — learning how pg_probackup handles incremental backups and what this means for database maintenance
WAL file management — understanding how pg_probackup interacts with PostgreSQL's write-ahead logging (WAL) system

knowledge.what_it_is: Backup validation procedures ensure that PostgreSQL database backups are complete, consistent, and usable for recovery purposes.
what_you_will_learn: Backup formats — understanding the differences between pg_dump, pg_dumpall, and custom formats like JSON or CSV
Backup verification — using tools like pg_verifybackup to check backup integrity and consistency
Restore testing — verifying that backups can be restored successfully using pg_restore or other tools
Backup retention policies — implementing strategies for managing backup storage and retention periods
Point-in-time recovery (PITR) validation — ensuring that PITR works correctly with PostgreSQL backups
Wal-E integration — validating Wal-E's ability to manage WAL files and backups in a PostgreSQL environment
pg_basebackup validation — testing the integrity of pg_basebackup-generated backups

knowledge.what_it_is: Shell scripts are a set of commands written in a shell language (e.g., Bash, Zsh) that automate repetitive tasks on Unix-like systems, including PostgreSQL database management and maintenance.
what_you_will_learn: Shebang — specifying the interpreter to use for the script (e.g., #!/bin/bash)
Variables — declaring and using shell variables, including environment variables and positional parameters
Conditional statements — using if-else statements to control script flow based on conditions
Loops — iterating over files, directories, or other data structures using for-loops or while-loops
Functions — defining reusable blocks of code that can be called from anywhere in the script
Error handling — using try-catch blocks and exit codes to handle errors and exceptions
File input/output — reading and writing files using redirection operators (e.g., <, >, >>)
Process management — running background processes, managing process IDs, and killing processes

knowledge.what_it_is: Programming languages are used to write instructions that a computer can execute, and PostgreSQL supports various languages for scripting and automation.
what_you_will_learn: SQL — Structured Query Language: writing database operations using declarative syntax
PL/pgSQL — Procedural Language/PostgreSQL: writing stored procedures and functions in SQL-like syntax
Python — popular language used for data analysis, machine learning, and automation with libraries like psycopg2 and SQLAlchemy
Perl — scripting language used for system administration and automation tasks with DBI and DBD::Pg drivers
Tcl — embedded language used for PostgreSQL extensions and plugins with the Tcl-PG interface
JavaScript — used for client-side scripting and server-side programming with Node.js and libraries like pg and pg-promise
Lua — lightweight language used for game development, embedded systems, and PostgreSQL extensions with the Lua-PG interface

knowledge.what_it_is: Salt is an open-source automation and configuration management tool, written in Python, used to manage infrastructure as code (IaC) and automate tasks on Linux/Unix systems. It's widely adopted in DevOps environments for its scalability, flexibility, and ease of use.
what_you_will_learn: SaltStack — the core Salt framework, including the Master, Minion, and Controller components
SLS (State) files — defining infrastructure as code using YAML or JSON syntax
Pillar data — storing sensitive information in a hierarchical structure for use in SLS files
Grains — system-specific attributes used to target specific nodes or groups of nodes
Salt formulas — reusable templates for automating tasks and deploying applications
Salt states — defining the desired state of a node, including configuration and package management
Salt modules — custom Python code for extending Salt's functionality
Salt API — interacting with Salt using RESTful APIs or command-line tools

knowledge.what_it_is: Ansible is an open-source automation tool used to configure and manage IT resources, including PostgreSQL databases, using a declarative syntax.
what_you_will_learn: Playbooks — Ansible's configuration files that define the desired state of infrastructure, including PostgreSQL database configurations
Inventory — managing hosts and groups in Ansible, essential for targeting specific PostgreSQL servers or clusters
Modules — reusable Ansible code snippets that perform specific tasks, such as creating databases, users, or granting privileges on PostgreSQL
Roles — organizing playbooks and modules into reusable templates for consistent configuration across multiple PostgreSQL environments
Handlers — running arbitrary commands or scripts during playbook execution, useful for customizing PostgreSQL settings or applying patches
Templates — using Ansible's Jinja2 templating engine to generate dynamic configuration files for PostgreSQL, such as pg_hba.conf or postgresql.conf
Variables and Facts — understanding how to use Ansible's built-in variables and facts to customize playbook execution and PostgreSQL configurations

knowledge.what_it_is: A simple stateful setup in PostgreSQL involves configuring the database to persist data across sessions and manage connections efficiently.
what_you_will_learn: pg_settings — managing PostgreSQL configuration parameters, such as shared_buffers and effective_cache_size
postgresql.conf — editing the main PostgreSQL configuration file for custom settings
pg_ctl — using the command-line utility to start, stop, and restart the PostgreSQL server
service management — configuring system services to manage PostgreSQL startup and shutdown
connection pooling — implementing connection pooling using pg_bouncer or pgbouncer
pg_stat_activity — monitoring active sessions and connections for performance optimization
pg_settings vs. postgresql.conf — understanding the difference between configuration parameters and file-based settings
customizing pg_hba.conf — configuring PostgreSQL authentication settings for remote access

knowledge.what_it_is: Chef is an open-source infrastructure as code (IaC) tool used to manage and automate the configuration of PostgreSQL servers, ensuring consistency and repeatability across environments.
what_you_will_learn: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — managing PostgreSQL server configurations using code instead of manual intervention
Chef Recipes — writing cookbooks that define the desired state of PostgreSQL servers, including resource creation and configuration
Resources — defining and managing PostgreSQL resources such as databases, users, and roles using Chef's built-in resource types
Attributes — configuring PostgreSQL attributes like listen_address, port, and authentication settings using Chef's attribute system
LWRPs (Lightweight Resource Providers) — creating custom LWRPs to manage complex PostgreSQL tasks like database backups and restores
Chef InSpec — using Chef's compliance tool to audit and enforce PostgreSQL security configurations
Test Kitchen — automating the testing of Chef cookbooks and recipes for PostgreSQL environments

knowledge.what_it_is: Puppet is an open-source configuration management tool that automates the deployment, configuration, and management of infrastructure resources such as servers, networks, and databases. It uses a declarative syntax to define desired states and enforces compliance through automated testing and remediation.
what_you_will_learn: Modules — reusable collections of Puppet code for common tasks like package management and file deployment
Resources — abstract representations of system resources such as files, users, and groups that can be managed by Puppet
Classes — reusable blocks of Puppet code that encapsulate related configuration settings and behaviors
Types and Providers — defining custom data types and providers to interact with specific systems or services
PuppetDB — a database for storing and querying Puppet-related metadata and configuration data
Hiera — a data storage and retrieval system for managing external data sources such as environment variables and user input
